[[RunningHead]]

The Omnipotence of the Psychoanalyst: Thoughts on the Need to Consider Retirement

Martin Nass

Martin Nass

40 Fifth Avenue, Apt. 4A
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: mn11@nyu.edu

Martin Nass   Clinical Professor and faculty (Emeritus), NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; Training and Supervisory Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society; Professor Emeritus, Brooklyn College, City University of New York.

O let not Time deceive you, You cannot conquer Time.

—W. H. Auden

The issue of retirement in the psychoanalytic profession is a rather delicate and usually unspoken matter. In fact, a recent paper by Norman Clemens (2011a) is titled “A Psychiatrist Retires: An Oxymoron?” On many occasions nonpsychoanalytic colleagues and friends have asked about my own plans for retirement, but I cannot think of one instance when a psychoanalytic colleague asked me when I was planning to retire. It is a question that had never occurred to me until rather recently. I had thought that my practice would eventually close through attrition. In fact, over the past several years I was working only twenty to twenty-five treatment hours a week. I had stopped accepting new analytic patients about fifteen years ago, except for some time-limited reanalyses, and was still working with some long-term analytic patients and several psychotherapy patients, as well as a few candidates and graduates in supervision. Finally, though, a series of noncatastrophic personal and family medical problems made me realize that it was time to formally end my psychoanalytic practice of fifty years. Analysts apparently plan to keep working until they are no longer able to function—and here lies the major problem. There are isolated stories of their continuing to work past the age of one hundred. More frequent are stories of elderly analysts or supervisors falling asleep, forgetting important material about the patient, and keeping the focus of these errors on the patient.

1013

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to the APsaA University Forums

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

E-mail: sjcoenmd@aol.com

Stanley Coen  

You are about to have the pleasure of reading a wonderfully integrative essay on discussions of Henry James's The Portrait of a Lady from the January 2014 University Forum at the ApsaA meetings in New York. University Forums aim to bridge academia and psychoanalysis. The Portrait of a Lady was discussed by Colm Tóibín, novelist, short story writer, playwright, and critic, by Jean Strouse, biographer, critic, and director of the Cullman Center for Scholars and Writers at the New York Public Library, and by Shelley Orgel and Lucy LaFarge, esteemed psychoanalysts and avid Jamesians. Leslie Keith, the essay's author, brings literary knowledge and skill to her writing (she holds a Ph.D. in English language and literature from the University of Chicago), as well as an enthusiasm for psychoanalysis. You will see her shift thoughtfully between literary and psychoanalytic perspectives. Her excitement about writing and about psychoanalysis will prove contagious for her readers.

Since Bonnie Litowitz's early involvement in developing the University Forums at APsaA, she has encouraged JAPA to delight in multidisciplinary writing. She found Leslie Keith (a candidate at the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis) and encouraged her participation in the January 2014 forum. Keith's essay is not a report but a commentary on James, Tóibín, Strouse, Orgel, and LaFarge, and on the aims and goals of novels and of psychoanalysis. I hope it will be followed by similar essays on this January's forum—“Terrorism/Violence,” with Jessica Stern, author of Terror in the Name of God: Why Religious Militants Kill, Stephen Xenakis, retired brigadier general, psychiatrist, and psychological evaluator of Guantanamo “terrorist” detainees, and the psychoanalysts M. Gerard Fromm and Jane Tillman—and on two planned for June 2015 and January 2016. “The Unseen/Unheard: A Social Science Perspective” will be held in San Francisco in June, with the South African sociologist Pumla Gobodo-Madikizela, author of A Human Being Died

[[RunningHead]]

Boundaries and Bridges: Perspectives on Time and Space in Psychoanalysis. By Andrea Sabbadini. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xxiv + 162 pp., $ 37.95 paperback.

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

71 Llanfair Circle
Ardmore, PA 19003-3342
E-mail: salman.akhtar@jefferson.edu

Salman Akhtar   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia; Professor of Psychiatry, Jefferson Medical College.

The clinical work of psychoanalysis takes place in the realm of imagination but is grounded in sober reality. It seems timeless and yet a clock keeps its stern eye over the proceedings. It never moves out of a fixed location, even

819

while encouraging mental travels far beyond the plebeian strictures of such space. Boundaries are maintained, but talk of transgression is pervasive. the work necessitates profound intimacy but “must be carried out in abstinence” (Freud 1915a, p. 165). Two notable vectors within such clash and contrast are those of time and space, elements that are the basis of Andrea Sabbadini's book. A London-based psychoanalyst of Italian origin and former film review editor of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, Sabbadini (2003, 2007) is better known for his work in the realm of psychoanalysis and cinema. I therefore welcome the opportunity to review his work on matters pertaining to clinical conceptualization and practice.

Sabbadini's book addresses a number of topics: The development of a sense of time, the phenomenon of the replacement child, virginity, the timelessness of the unconscious and seemingly of the analytic treatment, the turn of the century and the emotional significance of the year 2000, use of the couch, “possession anxiety,” silence, sounds, and creativity. Each topic is explored with the novel addition of a spatiotemporal perspective. Each is highlighted with opposing conceptualizations and then given a fresh nuance by the “layering of significance” (Kristeva 1987, p. 6) that such conceptualizations embody. the development of time, for instance, is seen as initially being split between a deeply personal time (the “infantile omnipresent”) and the universal time governed by clocks and calendars. This proposal is followed by the idea that psychic maturity necessitates “the achievement of a balance” (p. 5) between these two temporalities. the widely accepted axiom that the system Ucs. is timeless is accepted, but its absoluteness is questioned (p. 11). One is also reminded that the system Cs., too, can become oblivious to the passage of time (e.g., during the experience of falling in love or suffering severe physical pain).

Wrestling with the Angel: Experiments in Symbolic Life. By Tracy McNulty. New York: Columbia University Press, 2014, 300 pp., $30.00 paperback.

T. Johnson

T. Johnson

120 Norman Mayer Hall
Tulane University
New Orleans, LA 70117
E-mail: trj@tulane.edu

T. Johnson   Professor of English and Weiss Presidential Fellow, Tulane University.

Tracy McNulty's new book, Wrestling with the Angel: Experiments in Symbolic Life, addresses the growing sense that freedom, in contemporary thought, is too often defined negatively—that is, as the opposite of social norms and laws. By emphasizing too simplistically what freedom opposes, she claims, we miss the crucial way that freedom is in fact not opposed to such restraints but is rather enabled by them. in this light, restraints can function as indispensable resources for creativity and social change. They are essential to freedom—and desire—the very ground of their possibility, what freedom/desire works on and with and through. Though we tend to see this point rather easily in conversations about poetry and music and in the context of teaching and learning, we often miss it, says McNulty, in our thinking about social and legal issues.

When we look at legal constraints alongside formal constraints (those of poetry or music), we can see, suggests McNulty, the crucial difference between the letter of the law and its prescriptive function as representation of some law-making authority or identificatory ego-ideal. For McNulty, the letter of the law, most simply, is its liberating dimension. Consider for example the way Raymond Queneau describes a strict adherence to the precise letter of the various limits that, in his writing, he forces himself to observe: such a devotion protects him from “inspiration,” an utterly random business of jotting down whatever phrases happen to “come knocking at the window,” which in fact is best understood only as the writer's obedience, even slavery, to rules that remain unknown. Instead of involuntary submission to blind whimsy, his work proceeds as a rigorous activity of will, of desire, of grappling.

825

Unrepresented States and the Construction of Meaning: Clinical and Theoretical Contributions. Edited by Howard B. Levine, Gail S. Reed, and Dominique Scarfone. London: Karnac Books, 2013, xvi + 287 pp., $42.95 paperback.

Stanley Possick

Stanley Possick

102 Marvel Road
New Haven, CT 06515
E-mail: stanley.possick@yale.edu

Stanley Possick   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine.

Unrepresented States and the Construction of Meaning comprises twelve sophisticated, often complex essays, all well-written and very informative. the book is dedicated to the memory of André Green, and in an

835

introductory chapter the editors—Gail Reed, Howard Levine, and Dominique Scarfone—describe Green's understanding of the primordial mind. They describe too the theoretical and technical innovations he believed were required in undertaking the psychoanalytic treatment of nonneurotic, borderline, and often severely traumatized individuals who lack, in whole or in part, a capacity for self- and object representation and who have a related absence of psychic structure. Green presented his views on the psychoanalytic treatment of these “borderline” patients during an historic debate with Leo Rangell on the issue of change in psychoanalysis at the 1975 meetings of the International Psychoanalytical Association. Each of the authors in this volume touches, directly or indirectly, on representation or its absence in Green's work, or in their own. the editors espouse the position taken by Green forty years ago when he argued that psychoanalysts needed a new set of basic assumptions regarding work with borderline patients, and had to modify their clinical expectations, their techniques, and the analytic setting in order to treat them successfully. With this volume the editors seek to present these ideas, their subsequent development, and evidence of their clinical utility to North American analysts, many of whom might not yet have been exposed to Green's groundbreaking innovations or to the work of others currently grappling with the vicissitudes of understanding and treating patients who lack an adequate capacity for representation.

This volume's introductory chapter provides a superb explication of Green's theoretical postulates and his approach to psychoanalytic work with these patients. as one of those for whom Green's work was unfamiliar, I found this chapter essential reading and will summarize it, with a liberal use of quotes from Green and the editors.

[[RunningHead]]

Women and Creativity: A Psychoanalytic Glimpse Through Art, Literature, and Social Structure. Edited by Laura Tognoli Pasquali and Frances Thomson-Salo. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xxviii + 334 pp., $ 32.71 paperback.

Angela Cappiello

Angela Cappiello

49 Welles Street, Suite 216
Glastonbury, CT 06033
E-mail: angelacappiello3@aol.com

Angela Cappiello   Member, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Dictionaries define the word create as “to bring into existence.” Creativity is the encounter (from the Latin in and contra, against) between two entities that creates something new, and the concept implies a dynamism between two poles.

843

Despite their title, Women and Creativity, the editors, Laura Tognoli Pasquali and Frances Thomson-Salo, specify that creativity is not a prerogative of either women or men; rather, it “belongs to a couple”: “man and woman but [also] … mother and baby, … father and mother, teacher and pupil … all potentially creative couples able to produce all kinds of creative links: a shape and a thought, a word and an idea, an experience and an emotion, a colour and an image” (p. xix).

The ideas elaborated in this book were conceived at a conference held in Genoa in 2013 by psychoanalysts brought together to discuss the creative process.

The psychoanalytic literature is familiar with the concept of bisexuality and creativity (Ferraro 2003; Parker 1998). Joyce McDougall (1993) describes all creative acts as a fusion of the masculine and feminine elements in the psychic structure and believes that a lack of integration and acceptance of both poles of our psychic bisexuality may trigger creative paralysis.

So if creativity belongs to a couple, why the title Women and Creativity? the editors answer that this book is about women because often, especially in some cultures, and even in psychoanalysis, there has been a dismissive attitude toward female mental and biological creativity. the editors believe that too often, creativity and generativity have been confused and devalued. Generativity is dealt with as a concrete fact, deprived of any symbolic meaning or value in promoting the development of female psychology. Tognoli Pasquali and Thomson-Salo note that “we cannot speak about women's creativity while ignoring the presence of the womb, this inside presence the unconscious phantasy of which inevitably forges a woman's development and, we believe, that of the man, too” (p. xx).

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

For the Love of Death: Somnophilic and Necrophilic Acts and Fantasies

Danielle Knafo

Danielle Knafo

720 Northern Boulevard
Brookville, NY 11548-1300
E-mail: danielleknafo@gmail.com

Danielle Knafo  

The psychoanalytic literature on somnophilic and necrophilic fantasies and acts is indeed sparse. It is argued here that one must distinguish the forms of fantasy, playacting, and actual behavior that lie along a spectrum of romantic or sexual behavior involving people who are sleeping, drugged, immobile, inanimate, comatose, or dead. Regardless of these distinctions, however, the underlying dynamics commonly involve reunion with the mother, an inability to mourn, fear of the female, and an attempt at mastering and transcending the fear of death. To highlight these psychodynamics, a clinical case of somnophilia, Pedro Almodóvar's Talk to Her (a film about romantic relationships with comatose women), and the case of Count von Cosel, a quixotic necrophiliac, are presented. Gender differences are emphasized in the analysis of case material and cultural tropes.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Treatment for Separation Anxiety in a Treatment Nonresponder

Fredric Busch and Barbara Milrod

Barbara Milrod

10 East 78th Street, #5A
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: fnb80@aol.com

Barbara Milrod  

psychoanalytic research, manualized psychoanalytic psychotherapy, separation anxiety, defense mechanisms, transference interpretation

Separation anxiety, long an area of interest for psychoanalysts, has been included in DSM-5 among general “anxiety disorders” that span across age groups. The syndrome of separation anxiety has been shown to correlate with nonresponse to treatments for anxiety and mood disorders (Milrod et al. 2014). It is therefore of public health importance to develop targeted treatments for this syndrome. Some psychoanalysts have suggested that brief psychoanalytic interventions may be of particular value in addressing separation anxiety. Our clinical work with patients with anxiety disorders with high levels of separation anxiety indicates that they have such intense anger and ambivalence in fraught intimate relationships that they feel stuck and helpless, almost eliminating more positive feelings. This ambivalence and associated unconscious conflicts inevitably emerge in the therapeutic relationship and can threaten to disrupt treatment efforts. We propose a set of focused psychodynamic psychotherapeutic interventions to address separation anxiety, developed as part of Panic-Focused Psychodynamic Psychotherapy-eXtended Range (PFPP-XR; Busch et al. 2012). We present a case from our research study of treatment nonresponders with anxiety disorders and separation anxiety. The patient was successfully treated with PFPP-XR in a 21-session treatment.

An Innovative Brief Psychodynamic Psychotherapy: Commentary on Busch and Milrod

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605
E-mail: okernber@med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg  

Busch and Milrod's presentation summarizes the brief psychodynamic psychotherapy of a patient suffering from an anxiety disorder with panic attacks, depression, and significant separation anxiety. It is a case from a treatment study using Panic Focused Psychodynamic Psychotherapy-eXtended Range, a specific psychodynamic psychotherapy for patients with separation anxiety and anxiety disorders developed by Milrod, Busch, and colleagues. (Milrod et al. 2007; Busch et al. 2012). This particular case was selected from a sample of treatment nonresponders with anxiety disorders and separation anxiety on the basis of having had three previous treatments to which he did not respond, and now was successfully treated with the present treatment in a 21-session trial.

This treatment uses sophisticated dynamic concepts regarding separation and attachment, particularly unconscious conflicts around rage toward ambivalently loved attachment objects, and the related fear of self-assertion and independence because of their aggressive and vengeful implications.

Director, Personality Disorders Institute, New York Presbyterian Hospital, Westchester Division; Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

responseSet.title: Hans Loewald's Psychoanalysis and the History of the Individual
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hans Loewald's Psychoanalysis and the History of the Individual

T. Downey

T. Downey

132 Sachems Head Road
Guilford, CT 06437
E-mail: twdowney@comcast.net

T. Downey   Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor of Child Psychiatry, Yale Child Study Center.

Hans Loewald's Psychoanalysis and the History of the Individual (1978), along with a companion piece, his final published essay, “Psychoanalysis in Search of Nature: Thoughts on Metapsychology, ‘Metaphysics,’ Projection” (1988), are important contributors to a summation of his thinking. They also offer a blueprint for envisioning the evolution of our understanding of the human psyche over the coming century. In the latter work, an overlooked crystallization of Loewald's many years of thinking about the critical and inextricable interrelationship of psychoanalysis and philosophy, he posits a major realignment of psychoanalytic theory. Here he has engineered his own sort of Copernican, or perhaps I should say “Loewaldian,” shift. Just as Copernicus proclaimed just before his death that the earth is not the center of our universe but rather that the universe is heliocentric, with the earth revolving around and being influenced by its dominant partner, the sun, so did Loewald postulate that the human psyche is no longer the center of the Freudian analytic cosmos. Man is no longer the center of a conscious/unconscious system, but shares these characteristics with a much more primary cosmic natural order. Loewald “marries” psychoanalysis to what I take to be a wider evolutionary model of nature: human nature not apart from nature, with nature as its projection, but as part of a natural evolutionary order in which external influences are taken into the psyche through introjection, identification, and an active process of co-modification of both the external and the internal milieu. These forces, a kind of psychological osmosis, are on this model equal to or greater than the anthropocentric forces of defensive projection that have traditionally been seen as unconsciously relocating the unwanted parts of ourselves out into the great blue yonder. Loewald emphasizes the primacy of unconscious creative-destructive activity in nature quite generally. Human consciousness, rather than being central to mental functioning, is characterized as a sporadic outgrowth of largely unconscious processes of life and death.

responseSet.title: Y.-Y. Tang, B.K. Hölzel, & M.I. Posner (2015). The neuroscience of mindfulness meditation. Nature Reviews Neuroscience 16:213-225.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Y.-Y. Tang, B.K. Hölzel, & M.I. Posner (2015). The neuroscience of mindfulness meditation.Nature Reviews Neuroscience16:213-225.

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi

Tiziano Colibazzi  

Do psychoanalytic treatment and mindfulness meditation have anything in common? One may reflexively answer in the negative. Yet the mental functions underlying mindfulness meditation and psychoanalytic process have some striking similarities, suggesting that there may be considerable overlap in the relevant neural circuitry. At the very least, consideration of insights provided by the study of the neural bases of meditation might yield benefits for psychoanalytic research.

Yi-Yuan Tang et al. review in detail the status of current neuroscientific research in mindfulness meditation, providing a critical overview of findings and making important methodological points along the way.

Implicated in different types of meditative practices (e.g., mantra, tai-chi, chi-gong, yoga), mindfulness meditation has received much attention over the last few years and has been incorporated into treatments such as integrated body-mind training, mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR), and related therapeutic interventions. Mindfulness is based on the Buddhist concept of equanimity, usually described as nonjudgmental attention to the present moment. Mindfulness-based practices rely either on focused attention or on open monitoring of experiences.

Neuroscientific evidence suggests that mindfulness meditation increases overall self-regulation through three mechanisms: increased attentional control; improved emotion regulation; and a modification of self-awareness.

Attentional control includes alerting, orienting, and conflict-monitoring. In beginners, mindfulness meditation appears to improve conflict-monitoring and orienting, while in more experienced practitioners alerting is enhanced. Functional and structural changes following meditation have been observed in regions known to support attentional control, specifically the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC).

[[RunningHead]]

Canvas of Change: Analysis through the Prism of Creativity. By Ilany Kogan. London: Karnac Books, 2012, xvi + 154 pp., $40.95 paperback.

Aisha Abbasi

Aisha Abbasi

5720 Bloomfield Glens
West Bloomfield, MI 48322
E-mail: abbasimd@sbcglobal.net

Aisha Abbasi   Training and Supervising Analyst, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute.

Ilany Kogan, a training analyst at the Israel Psychoanalytic Society, has written a book of luminous beauty. Kogan divides the book into three parts: Creativity through the Prism of Analysis; Analysis through the

1276

Prism of Stories from the Bible; and Analysis through the Prism of Paintings and Poems. I will discuss each of these in turn.

In the two chapters of Part I, Creativity through the Prism of Analysis, Kogan sketches out for us, first, a review of classical and contemporary literature regarding the sources and functions of creativity and, second, an understanding of creativity through the prism of various analytic models. Kogan tells us that Freud believed “that the artist, like everyone else, suffers from unfulfilled wishes that cannot be acknowledged because of their forbidden nature. Artists, however, escape a neurotic outcome of this conflict not just by the common sublimations available to ordinary people, but by a gift that enables them to give shape to the forbidden, unconscious fantasy and to express it visually or verbally in a way that avoids censorship” (pp. 3-4). She contrasts this with Melanie Klein's idea of creativity being used to achieve “reparation,” which was seen by Klein as a highly constructive urge involving the attempt to correct the effect of aggressive feelings and urges. Kogan tells us that Hanna Segal uses Kleinian ideas to discuss creativity as “a universal process of development…. an expression of the reparative impulse, which stems from the depressive position” (p. 4).

Kogan also shares with us her understanding of the work of Ernst Kris and Anton Ehrenzweig. Kris sees creativity as a “regression in the service of the ego,” depicting the work of a healthy ego that deals with painful early experiences through different modes of expression. Ehrenzweig, by contrast, views creative function not as regression in the service of the ego, but rather as progression (p. 5).

Kogan next surveys the work of analysts such as Joyce McDougall, Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel, and Madeleine Baranger, to name but a few who have “advanced the study of the creative process” (p. 6).

[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Institutionalized Children in a Developing Country: Exploration of Trauma and Attachment at an Orphanage in India

Ksera Dyette and Monisha Nayar-Akhtar

Monisha Nayar-Akhtar

412 North Orange Street, Apt. A5
Media, PA 19063
E-mail: ktdyette@gmail.com

Monisha Nayar-Akhtar  

Institutionalized children have lately been the subject of a number of researchers’ empirical endeavors (see, e.g., Dozier et al. 2011; St. Petersburg-USA Orphanage Research Team 2008). Yet despite their efforts, which confirm that institutional care is harmful to children, institutional care settings (orphanages) still remain the only option in some countries.

responseSet.title: Leon Hoffman on Lieberman's Shrinks
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Leon Hoffman on Lieberman's Shrinks

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: hoffman.leon@gmail.com

Leon Hoffman  

I would like to thank Richard C. Friedman for his positive comments about my review of Jeffrey Lieberman's Shrinks: The Untold Story of

NP34

Psychiatry. In essence, I agree with Friedman's additional commentary. I would like to highlight one point in his letter. He states that Lieberman populates the universe with ‘“heroes’ and ‘shrinks’ (biological versus psychodynamically oriented psychiatrists),” noting that this way of thinking “promotes divisiveness rather than bridge building.”

To me, Lieberman's depiction of “good guys” and “bad guys” is reflective of the all-too-human susceptibility of promoting identification with a particular group by casting out those who think differently. Those who violate the norms of the group are extruded from it. In fact, even young children may actively enforce group norms by extruding those who violate them (Schmidt, Rakoczy, and Tomasello 2012).

Lieberman stabilizes his identification as a biomédical psychiatrist by denying the contributions of those who do not subscribe to his credo. Obviously, a more scientific approach to the subject of psychiatric diagnosis and treatment would have involved a careful assessment of contributions from a range of theoretical perspectives. Lieberman's identification with his group seems more important to him than an objective, deliberative approach to the complex topic of mental disorders and mental health treatment.

Ever since the Enlightenment there have been periodic ebbs and flows of theories about the human mind and how to alleviate its suffering. Clearly, no one has yet found the “magic bullet” with which to address all of its complexities. None of us should delude ourselves into thinking that our pet theory is the only way to understand the mind or the only way to help those who suffer (Hoffman 2014, p. 143).

It is unfortunate that Jeffrey Lieberman, a leader in psychiatry, seems to believe that he has found that magic bullet. The problem with this approach is illustrated by his two case examples, in which he blames families who would not agree to the use of psychotropic medications for their disturbed family members. Lieberman truly seems convinced that his approach is the only approach and that if patients only listened to him their problems would disappear: a tragic and ultimately self-defeating approach.
